id: detecting web attacks/client-side vs server-side attacks
title: Client-side vs Server-side attacks
relPath: Detecting Web Attacks/Client-side vs Server-side attacks.md

breadcrumbs.id: detecting web attacks/client-side vs server-side attacks
breadcrumbs.title: Client-side vs Server-side attacks

overview: Client-side attacks exploit the user’s browser/device or their actions. The attacker runs malicious code in the user’s environment to steal sessions, credentials, or data that lives on the endpoint.

study.overview: Client-side attacks exploit the user’s browser/device or their actions. The attacker runs malicious code in the user’s environment to steal sessions, credentials, or data that lives on the endpoint.
study.whySoc: Use this note as an analyst reference: understand the concept, identify the relevant telemetry, pivot through evidence, and document the decision path clearly.
workflow: Identify the user, host, process, network flow, or identity object involved.
Collect the highest-value logs first and preserve timestamps and assumptions.
Pivot through related notes, telemetry sources, and MITRE references.
Document findings, gaps, containment options, and detection improvements.
tips: WAFs are strong first-line mitigations but can be evaded by crafted payloads, custom tools, or encrypted traffic if TLS is not inspected. Use WAFs with secure coding, input validation, and proper logging.
